offer.what: Subscription-based 3D pixel-streaming virtual world platform with persistent districts, in-game economy (OCX currency), AI digital citizens (DigOzens), creator tools, event hosting, and cross-platform game publishing
offer.coreOutcome: A persistent digital identity and economic presence — a virtual city where users can live, work, build, and earn in an immersive cross-platform world
offer.pricePoint: Free (Explorer) → $12.99/mo (OmniFolk) → $39.99/mo (OmniPreneur) → OmniPro (early access, price TBD) → Producer (limited 10 licenses per game, price TBD)
offer.commitment: Monthly subscription, no annual option listed; free Explorer tier is perpetual

audience.icp: Gaming enthusiasts, content creators, and digital entrepreneurs aged 18–35 who want a meaningful virtual presence and economic opportunity inside an immersive world — early adopters disappointed by previous metaverse-era promises
painPoints: Existing metaverse platforms (Meta Horizon, Decentraland) failed to deliver real community or practical utility
No viable way to monetize creative skills inside virtual games without being a AAA studio
Fragmented gaming identity across platforms with no persistent virtual home base
High barrier to 3D creation — Omniverse explicitly lowers this (no prior experience required)
Virtual events and communities feel hollow with low real attendance
segments: Explorers: casual free users discovering the platform and attending events
Residents (OmniFolk): community members wanting a digital home, social presence, and in-game currency access
Virtual entrepreneurs (OmniPreneur): creators and small business owners monetizing shops, services, or experiences inside the world
Professional creators / publishers (OmniPro + Producer): indie studios and developers building and distributing games or large-scale experiences on the platform

salesCycle.length: Same-day for Explorer signup; days-to-weeks for upgrade to paid after experiencing the platform
salesCycle.trial: Yes — free Explorer tier is a perpetual limited-access free tier that acts as the trial
salesCycle.closeMechanism: Self-serve upgrade from within the platform; monetization, event hosting, and higher OCX allocations locked behind paid tiers create natural upgrade pressure
salesCycle.topObjection: Is this platform stable and long-term viable? Alpha/early-access status raises trust and longevity concerns — users hesitate to invest time or money in something that might shut down

ahaMoment: First meaningful session inside the virtual world — attending a live event, earning OCX through participation, or publishing a first shop/space — making the digital city feel real, inhabited, and worth committing to

retention.recurring: Monthly subscriptions across OmniFolk ($12.99), OmniPreneur ($39.99), and OmniPro tiers
retention.expansion: Tier upgrades (Explorer → OmniFolk → OmniPreneur → OmniPro → Producer); OCX spending on marketplace; revenue sharing as a Producer; affiliate/partner program
retention.churnRisk: Platform is in alpha — users disengage if update cadence slows, live events are sparse, or the peer community feels thin; monthly billing means zero switching cost

lifecycleGaps: Welcome & Onboarding — no evidence of structured email onboarding sequence for new Explorer signups
Lead-Magnet Nurture — free Explorer users need a nurture sequence converting them to OmniFolk or OmniPreneur
Trial/Consult Conversion — no clear conversion email flow from free to paid tier visible
Value Newsletter — blog content exists but no evidence of regular email distribution to subscribers
Renewal & Expansion — no evident upgrade-nudge or monthly engagement campaigns to push tier advancement
Win-Back / Re-Engagement — no evidence of lapsed-user reactivation flow for inactive Explorers or churned subscribers
toneVoice: Energetic, aspirational, community-first; inclusive framing (no prior 3D experience required); uses world-building language (Explorers, OmniFolk, OmniPreneur, districts, eventures, DigOzens); sense of pioneering — 'We Are All Players'; professional enough for entrepreneur segment but playful enough for gaming audience
compliance: Standard CAN-SPAM and GDPR apply; no HIPAA or financial-services constraints; in-game currency (OCX) should be described as platform credits, not investment instruments, to avoid regulatory issues
